text: The claim rests on several moderate pieces of evidence that Oso’s writing and poems address environmental and human‑rights topics, his endorsement of the Great Green Wall project, and his praise for a film exposing societal inaction, all of which suggest a willingness to challenge prevailing neglect. Stronger support comes from his explicit encouragement to question the unknown and his emphasis on human knowledge over prayer, both of which directly oppose conformist acceptance of established narratives. Additional moderate points—his belief that storytelling can reshape cultures and his call to eliminate carbon‑emitting activities—further reinforce a pattern of confronting conventional practices. Together, these statements collectively portray Oso as inclined toward non‑conformist attitudes.
